# Bayes' theorem with discrete models

Here is the whole idea of Bayesian inference in one sentence:
**start with what you already believe, then update it in proportion to how well the new data fit.**
Nothing more mysterious than that. The maths below is just careful bookkeeping
for that update.

You begin with a belief held *before* the data arrive — the **prior**. You ask how
well each explanation accounts for what you saw — the **likelihood**. Multiply the
two, tidy up so the numbers add to one, and you have your belief *after* the data
— the **posterior**.

## A fish that could have come from three reefs

Imagine you are tracking fish on a reef system with acoustic tags. A tagged fish
swims past a line of underwater receivers and leaves a trail of detections. From
the pattern of pings you want to know one thing:
**which reef did this fish come from — North, Mid, or South?**

The detections are not a clean answer. The same trail could plausibly have been
left by a fish from any of the three reefs — just with different odds. From years
of past tracking you know roughly how often *this particular* detection pattern
shows up for fish from each reef. Those three numbers are your **likelihoods**:
how well each reef explains the data.

## The theorem, term by term

$$
\underbrace{P(\text{reef} \mid \text{data})}_{\text{posterior}}
=
\frac{
  \overbrace{P(\text{data} \mid \text{reef})}^{\text{likelihood}}\;
  \overbrace{P(\text{reef})}^{\text{prior}}
}{
  \underbrace{\sum_{r} P(\text{data} \mid \text{reef}_r)\,P(\text{reef}_r)}_{\text{denominator}}
}
$$

It looks busier than it is. Read it as three small steps:

- The **numerator** for each reef is just `prior × likelihood` — your belief in
  that reef, weighted by how well it explains the trail.
- The **denominator** is *the sum of those numerators across all three reefs*. It
  is the total probability of seeing this trail at all, and dividing by it is what
  forces the three posteriors to add up to 1.

That second point is the part people overthink.
**The denominator is not some extra quantity you have to go and find — it is just the numerators you already worked out, added together.**

## Where does the prior come from? A survey weight

In fisheries and marine ecology, surveys are rarely spread evenly. Some reefs get
visited on most trips; others get a glance once a season. That uneven effort is
recorded as a **survey weight** — a number saying how much of your sampling went
where. It turns out to be a very natural prior.

Suppose the boat reached the North reef on 60% of trips, the Mid reef on 30%, and
the South reef on only 10%. Before you look at a single ping, you already have a
reason to lean North: that is simply where you spent most of your effort, so that
is where most of your tagged fish are likely to be. Setting
$P(\text{reef}) \propto \text{survey weight}$ writes that reasoning down honestly:
*"my prior is proportional to how often I actually looked there."*

The beauty is that the data still get their say. When the acoustic evidence points
clearly to one reef, it will overrule the survey weight and the posterior shifts.
When the evidence is murky, the posterior stays close to the survey weight — which
is exactly the cautious behaviour you want.

This pattern shows up everywhere in ecology:

| Prior | What it encodes |
|-------|----------------|
| Uniform ($P = 1/n$ for each option) | Complete ignorance — every option equally plausible |
| Survey weight | Sampling effort — some places watched more than others |
| Historical detection rate | Past experience about where animals turn up |

The point is that choosing a prior is *a modelling decision you make out loud*.
Calling it a survey weight makes that decision easy to explain and easy to defend.

## Your task

In the challenge below, fill in the numerator for each reef, then the
denominator (their sum), then each posterior. Then switch the prior and watch the
posterior move — same fish, same pings, different starting beliefs.
